name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلْأَنفَالِ ۖ قُلِ ٱلْأَنفَالُ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَصْلِحُوا۟ ذَاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ ۖ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١)
```

```english
They ask you concerning the anfaal. Say, ‘The anfaal belong to Allah and the Apostle.’ So be wary of Allah and settle your differences, and obey Allah and His Apostle, should you be faithful. (1)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَإِذَا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ زَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٢)
```

```english
The faithful are only those whose hearts tremble [with awe] when Allah is mentioned, and when His signs are recited to them, they increase their faith, and who put their trust in their Lord, (2)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣)
```

```english
maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them. (3)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا ۚ لَّهُمْ دَرَجَـٰتٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٤)
```

```english
It is they who are truly the faithful. They shall have ranks near their Lord, forgiveness and a noble provision. (4)
```

```arabic
كَمَآ أَخْرَجَكَ رَبُّكَ مِنۢ بَيْتِكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ لَكَـٰرِهُونَ (٥)
```

```english
As your Lord brought you out from your home with a just cause, a part of the faithful were indeed reluctant. (5)
```

```arabic
يُجَـٰدِلُونَكَ فِى ٱلْحَقِّ بَعْدَ مَا تَبَيَّنَ كَأَنَّمَا يُسَاقُونَ إِلَى ٱلْمَوْتِ وَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ (٦)
```

```english
They disputed with you concerning the truth after it had become clear, as if they were being driven towards death as they looked on. (6)
```

## (Commentary)

The context of the verses in this chapter indicates that it is Medinan, revealed after the Battle of Badr. It recounts some reports of Badr and mentions various matters related to *jihad* (الجهاد) — *the striving in the way of Allah* —, spoils of war (الغنائم), and the *anfal* (الأنفال) — *the properties that belong exclusively to Allah and His Messenger* — and similar topics. It also addresses matters concerning the Migration (الهجرة), with which the chapter concludes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"They ask you about the bounties. Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle.’"** (8:1) until the end of the verse. *Al-Anfal* (الأنفال) is the plural of *nafal* (نفل), which means an addition to something. For this reason, the terms *nafal* (النفل) and *nafilah* (النافلة) are applied to supererogatory acts of worship (التطوع) because they are an addition to the obligatory (الفريضة). The term *anfal* is also applied to what is called *fay’* (فيئاً) — *property acquired from disbelievers without a formal war* — which are things among properties that have no specific human owner, such as the peaks of mountains, the depths of valleys, ruined dwellings, villages whose inhabitants have perished, and the estate of one who has no heir, and other such things. It is as if they are an addition to what people possess, yet no one owns them; thus, they belong to Allah and His Messenger. It is also applied to the spoils of war (غنائم الحرب) as if they are an addition to what was intended by it; for the objective of war and military expeditions is victory over the enemies and their eradication. Once they are overcome and victory is achieved, the objective is realized, and the wealth captured by the fighters and the people they have taken captive are an addition to the primary purpose.

The word *dhat* (ذات) — *essence or self* — was originally the feminine form of *dhu* (ذا), meaning "possessor of," and it is among the words that must be followed by a genitive construction (اللازمة الإضافة). However, it became frequently used to refer to the thing itself, in the sense of that by which a thing is what it is. Thus, it is said: "the essence of man" (ذات الإنسان), meaning that by which a human is a human; and "the essence of Zayd" (ذات زيد), meaning the specific human soul (النفس) named Zayd. Originally, the phrase was "the soul possessing such-and-such acts" (النفس ذات أعمال كذا), then it was mentioned in isolation as "the possessor of acts" (ذات الأعمال) or its equivalent, and eventually simply as "essence" (ذات). The same applies to the phrase "the state of your relationship" (ذات بينكم). Since a dispute (الخصومة) cannot occur except between two parties, the "between-ness" (البين) was attributed to it, and it was called *dhat al-bayn* (ذات البين), meaning the state or the bad bond that possesses this "between-ness." Therefore, the meaning of His saying: أَصْلِحُوا ذاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ **"settle your differences"** (8:1) is: rectify the corrupt state and the bad bond that exists between you.

Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "*Dhu* (ذو) is used in two ways: one is to reach a description using generic nouns (أسماء الأجناس) and types (الأنواع); it is added to an explicit noun (الظاهر) but not to a pronoun (المضمر). It is dualized and pluralized; the dual is *dhawata* (ذواتا) and the plural is *dhawat* (ذوات), and none of these are used except in a genitive construction.

He said: The scholars of meanings (أصحاب المعاني) have borrowed the term *al-dhat* (الذات) and made it an expression for the identity of a thing (عين الشيء), whether it be a substance (جوهر) or an accident (عرض). They used it in isolation, added to a pronoun, and with the definite article. They treated it like the terms "soul" (النفس) and "particularity" (الخاصة), saying: "his essence" (ذاته), "his soul" (نفسه), and "his particularity" (خاصته). This is not from the original speech of the Arabs. The second way for the word *dhu* is a dialect of the Tayy tribe; they use it like the relative pronoun *al-ladhi* (الذي) — *who/which* — and it remains in one form for the nominative, accusative, and genitive cases, as well as for the plural and feminine, such as:

*And my well which (ذو) I dug and which (ذو) I lined.*

Meaning: *al-lati* (التي) I dug and *al-lati* I lined. (End of quote)."

What he mentioned regarding it not being added to a pronoun is narrated from al-Farra’, and its implication is that its use with a pronoun is from the speech of the later generations (المولدين). The truth is that it is rare, not abandoned; it has occurred in the speech of Ali (peace be upon him) in some of his sermons as found in *Nahj al-Balaghah*.

The commentators have differed greatly regarding the meaning of the verse and its context from several perspectives: regarding the meaning of His saying: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ **"They ask you about the bounties"** (8:1). It has been attributed to the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) and others like Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, and Talhah ibn Musarrif that they read it as: "They ask you [for] the bounties" (يسئلونك الأنفال) [without the preposition *'an*]. It was said that *'an* (عن) is extra in the famous recitation, and it was said that it is implied in the rare recitation. It was also said that *al-anfal* refers to the spoils of war (غنائم الحرب); or specifically the spoils of the Battle of Badr by making the definite article *al-* (ال) refer to a specific known instance (للعهد); or the *fay’* (الفيء) which belongs to Allah, the Messenger, and the Imam; or that the verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of the *khums* (الخمس) — *the one-fifth tax* —; or that it is decisive (محكمة). The dispute among them has been lengthy, as can be known by referring to the extensive commentaries like those of al-Razi, al-Alusi, and others.

What ought to be said, drawing support from the context, is that the verse by its context indicates that there was a specific dispute among those referred to by His saying: يَسْئَلُونَكَ **"They ask you"** (8:1), where some of them disputed with others, each taking a side that his opponent did not accept. The derivation found in His saying: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَصْلِحُوا ذاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ **"so be wary of Allah and settle your differences"** (8:1) indicates that the dispute was regarding the matter of the *anfal*. The implication of this is that the question they asked, recounted at the beginning of the verse, was only to settle the dispute. It is as if they disputed over the *anfal* and then referred to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asking him about its ruling so that the dispute might be settled by his answer and the conflict between them removed.

This—as you can see—supports, firstly, the famous recitation: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ **"They ask you about the bounties"** (8:1), for when the verb "to ask" (السؤال) is followed by the preposition *'an* (عن), it means seeking knowledge of a ruling or information. However, when it is used transitively by itself, it means a request or entreaty (الاستعطاف), and only the first meaning suits this context.

Secondly: Although the concept of *anfal* encompasses both spoils (الغنيمة) and *fay’* (الفيء), the specific occasion of the verse (مورد الآية) is the *anfal* in the sense of spoils of war, not specifically the spoils of the Battle of Badr alone. There is no reason for such specification; for when they disputed over the spoils of Badr, they did not dispute over them because they were specifically the spoils of Badr, but because they were spoils taken from the enemies of the religion in a religious *jihad*, which is evident.

The fact that the verse is specific to spoils of war in its occasion does not necessitate restricting the ruling contained therein to that occasion, for the occasion does not restrict the general ruling (المورد لا يخصص). Thus, the general application of the verse's ruling to everything called *nafal* is appropriate. It indicates that all *anfal* belong to Allah and His Messenger; none of the believers share them with Allah and His Messenger, whether it be spoils or *fay’*.

Furthermore, what is apparent from His saying: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1) and the exhortation Allah gives them after this sentence, urging them toward faith (الإيمان), is that Allah the Glorified settled the dispute by legislating its ownership for Himself and His Messenger, and removing it from their hands. This requires that their dispute was due to a group among them claiming that the *anfal* belonged to them specifically to the exclusion of others, or that they were entitled to a specific part of it, while the other group denied that. So Allah the Glorified settled their dispute by stripping them of ownership and establishing the ownership of Himself and His Messenger, and by admonishing them to cease the dispute and quarreling. As for the claim of those who say that the warriors own what they take of the spoils by consensus (الإجماع), it is more appropriate to discuss this in jurisprudence (الفقه) rather than exegesis (التفسير).

In short, their dispute over the *anfal* reveals a prior understanding they had that the spoils belonged to them, or something to that effect, except that it was an ambiguous ruling over which the disputants differed, each pulling the fire toward his own loaf of bread. The noble verses support this.

To clarify: the connection of the verses in the chapter and the explicit mention of the story of the Battle of Badr within it reveals that the entire chapter was revealed concerning the Battle of Badr and its immediate aftermath, to the extent that Ibn Abbas—according to what is narrated from him—used to call it "Surah Badr." The verses that address the matter of spoils are five verses in three places in the chapter, according to the order of the chapter: His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"They ask you about the bounties. Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1); and His saying, the Exalted: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ آمَنْتُمْ بِاللهِ وَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ **"Know that whatever thing you may come by as spoil, a fifth of it belongs to Allah and to the Apostle, and to the relatives, the orphans, the needy and the traveller, if you have faith in Allah and what We sent down to Our servant on the Day of Separation, the day when the two hosts met; and Allah has power over all things."** (8:41); and His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ لِنَبِيٍّ أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُ أَسْرى حَتَّى يُثْخِنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ تُرِيدُونَ عَرَضَ الدُّنْيا وَاللهُ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ وَاللهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ لَوْ لا كِتابٌ مِنَ اللهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيما أَخَذْتُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلالاً طَيِّباً وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ **"A prophet may not take captives until he has thoroughly decimated [the enemy] in the land. You desire the transitory goods of this world, while Allah desires the Hereafter [for you]; and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. Had it not been for a prior decree from Allah, there would have surely befallen you a great punishment for what you took. So eat of what you have taken as spoil, such as is lawful and good, and be wary of Allah; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful."** (8:67-69).

The context of the second verse (8:41) indicates that it was revealed after the first verse and the final verses altogether, because of His saying in it: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ آمَنْتُمْ بِاللهِ وَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ **"if you have faith in Allah and what We sent down to Our servant on the Day of Separation, the day when the two hosts met"** (8:41); thus, it was revealed some time after the battle.

Furthermore, the final verses (8:67-69) indicate that they spoke to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the matter of the captives and asked him not to kill them but to take ransom (الفدية). These verses contain a reproach for that, followed by the permission to eat from what they had taken as spoil. It seems they understood from this that they owned the spoils and the *anfal*, though with some ambiguity: did everyone who attended the battle own it, or only some, such as the fighters to the exclusion of those who stayed behind? And did they own it equally, such that it would be divided equally among them, or would they differ in it by increase or decrease, such as the share of the cavalry being more than the infantry? Or something similar.

This was the cause of the dispute among them, so they quarreled over the matter and referred it to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Then the first verse was revealed: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَصْلِحُوا ذاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ **"Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle.’ So be wary of Allah and settle your differences"** (8:1). The verse corrected them in their assumption that they were the owners of the *anfal* based on what they had understood from His saying: فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ **"So eat of what you have taken as spoil"** (8:69). It established the ownership of the *anfal* for Allah and the Messenger and forbade them from disputing and quarreling. When their dispute was thus ended, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned it to them and divided it among them equally. He set aside shares for a number of his companions who did not attend the battle, and he did not favor a fighter over one who stayed behind, nor a horseman over a footman. Then the second verse was revealed: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ **"Know that whatever thing you may come by as spoil, a fifth of it belongs to Allah"** (8:41) after some time, so the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) took the fifth from the shares he had returned to them, and the remainder stayed with them. This is what is gathered from joining the verses related to the *anfal* with one another.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ **"They ask you about the bounties"** (8:1) indicates, when joined with the contextual clues, that they asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about the ruling on spoils of war after they had assumed they owned the spoils, and they differed over who owned them, or how they were owned and divided among them, or both, and they disputed over that.

His saying: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1) is the answer to their question. It contains a clarification that they do not own them; rather, they are *anfal* owned by Allah and His Messenger, to be placed wherever Allah and His Messenger wish. This cut off the root of the disagreement and dispute that had broken out between them.

It appears from this explanation that the verse does not abrogate (غير ناسخة) His saying, the Exalted: فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ **"So eat of what you have taken as spoil"** (8:69) until the end of the verse; rather, it clarifies its meaning through interpretation (التفسير). His saying: فَكُلُوا **"So eat"** is not a metaphor for their ownership of the spoils in principle; rather, what is meant is the right of disposal (التصرف) and enjoyment (التمتع) thereof, unless they come to own it through the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) division of it among them.

It also appears that His saying, the Exalted: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى **"Know that whatever thing you may come by as spoil, a fifth of it belongs to Allah and to the Apostle, and to the relatives"** (8:41) is not an abrogator (ليس بناسخ) of His saying: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1). For His saying: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ **"Know that whatever thing you may come by as spoil"** (8:41) only affects the fighters by preventing them from eating the entirety of the spoils and disposing of all of it, since after the revelation of الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle"** (8:1), they had nothing but that [permission to eat]. As for His saying: الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle"** (8:1), it only establishes that the origin of its ownership (أصل ملكها) belongs to Allah and the Messenger, without addressing the manner of disposal or the permissibility of eating and enjoyment. Thus, His saying: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ **"Know that whatever thing you may come by as spoil"** (8:41) does not contradict it such that it would be an abrogator. Consequently, what is gathered from the three verses combined is: that the origin of ownership in the spoils belongs to Allah and the Messenger, then four-fifths of it return to the fighters who eat and possess it, and one-fifth returns to Allah, the Messenger, the relatives, and others, who have the right of disposal and exclusive right to it.

It also appears through reflection on the previous explanation that expressing the spoils as *anfal* (الأنفال)—which is the plural of *nafal* meaning "addition"—contains an allusion to justifying the ruling by its broader subject matter. It is as if it were said: "They ask you about the spoils, and they are additions (زيادات) that have no owner among the people. Since this is the case, answer them with the ruling of additions and *anfal*, and say: 'The *anfal* belong to Allah and the Messenger.'" The implication of this is that the spoils belong to Allah and the Messenger.

By this, it is perhaps supported that the definite article *al-* (ال) in the first instance of the word *al-anfal* is for a specific reference (للعهد) and in the second instance is for the genus (للجنس) or exhaustiveness (الاستغراق). This also clarifies the reason for the explicit mention in His saying: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ **"Say, ‘The bounties...’"** (8:1) where He did not say: "Say, they (هي) belong to Allah and the Messenger."

It also appears from this that His saying: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1) is a general ruling that encompasses by its generality the spoils and all other additional properties in society, similar to vacant dwellings, perished villages, mountain peaks, valley depths, the estates of kings, and the inheritance of one who has no heir. As for the *anfal* in the sense of spoils, they were assigned to the Muslim fighters by the action of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), while the rest remained under the ownership of Allah and His Messenger.

This is what reflection on the noble verses yields. The commentators have various opinions regarding them, which can be known by referring to the extensive commentaries; there is no benefit in recounting them or engaging in their refutation and confirmation here.

**His saying, the Exalted:** إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِرَ اللهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ **"The faithful are only those whose hearts tremble when Allah is mentioned"** (8:2) until the end of the two verses. These two verses and the one following them are a clarification of what distinguishes the believers who possess the reality of faith (حقيقة الإيمان) and the noble attributes and abundant reward they are characterized by. This was clarified to reinforce what is contained in His saying, the Exalted: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَصْلِحُوا ذاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ **"so be wary of Allah and settle your differences"** (8:1) until the end of the verse.

Allah the Exalted has mentioned five attributes for them, which He chose from among all their attributes mentioned in His speech, because they necessitate their noble qualities despite their abundance and are inseparable from the truth of faith. They are such that if they become mindful of them and reflect upon them, it would facilitate for them the disciplining of the soul toward godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure* —, settling their differences, and obeying Allah and His Messenger.

Those five attributes are: the trembling of the heart (وجل القلب) when Allah is mentioned, the increase of faith when listening to the verses of Allah, reliance (التوكل) — *the absolute trust in Allah as the sole disposer of affairs* —, the establishment of prayer (الصلاة), and spending from what Allah has provided them. It is well known that the first three attributes are acts of the heart (أعمال القلوب), and the last two are acts of the limbs (أعمال الجوارح).

In mentioning them, the natural order between them has been observed. For the light of faith only shines upon the heart gradually; it continues to intensify and multiply until it becomes complete and perfect in its reality. The first thing that shines is the heart's being affected by trembling and awe (الخشية) when it is reminded of Allah upon His mention, which is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِرَ اللهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ **"The faithful are only those whose hearts tremble when Allah is mentioned"** (8:2).

Then faith continues to expand, take root, grow, and branch out by journeying through the verses that point to Him, the Exalted, and guide to the true gnoses (المعارف الحقة). Whenever the believer reflects on any of them, it increases him in faith; thus, faith grows stronger and intensifies until it settles in the stage of certainty (اليقين), which is His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُهُ زادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً **"and when His signs are recited to them, they increase them in faith"** (8:2).

When faith increases and reaches perfection, he then recognizes the station of his Lord and the position of his own self—a recognition that corresponds to the reality of the matter, which is that the entire affair belongs to Allah the Glorified. For He, the Exalted, alone is the Lord (الرب) to Whom everything returns. Thus, the true duty upon man is to rely upon Him and follow what He wants from him by taking Him as a guardian (وكيلاً) in all that concerns him in his life. He becomes pleased with what He decrees for him in the course of life, and he proceeds according to the rulings He judges for him and the laws He legislates. Thus, he carries out His commands and refrains from His prohibitions, which is His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ **"and who put their trust in their Lord"** (8:2).

Then, when faith settles in its perfection in the heart, it necessitates that the servant turns with servitude (العبودية) toward his Lord, placing himself in the station of servitude and sincere submission (الخضوع), which is the prayer (الصلاة). This is a matter between him and his Lord. He also attends to the needs of society in the deficiencies of their endeavors by spending on the poor from what Allah has provided him of wealth, knowledge, or otherwise. This is a matter between him and the other members of his society, which is His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ يُنْفِقُونَ **"those who maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them"** (8:3).

It has become clear from what preceded that His saying, the Exalted: زادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً **"they increase them in faith"** (8:2) is an allusion to the increase in terms of quality (الكيفية), which is intensification and perfection, rather than quantity (الكمية), which is the increase in the number of believers, as some commentators have conjectured.

**His saying, the Exalted:** أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا لَهُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ **"It is they who are truly the faithful. They shall have ranks with their Lord, and forgiveness, and a noble provision."** (8:4). This is a judgment from Him, the Exalted, establishing the reality of faith in whoever is characterized by the five attributes He enumerated. Therefore, He left unrestricted the noble reward He mentioned for them in His saying: لَهُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ **"They shall have ranks with their Lord"** (8:4). Thus, these people possess the attributes of perfection, noble recompense, and great reward that belong to every true believer.

As for His saying: لَهُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ **"They shall have ranks with their Lord, and forgiveness, and a noble provision"** (8:4), forgiveness (المغفرة) is the divine pardoning of their sins, and the noble provision (الرزق الكريم) is the blessings of Paradise they are provided with. Allah the Glorified has intended by "noble provision" Paradise and its blessings in several places in His speech, such as His saying, the Exalted: فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ وَالَّذِينَ سَعَوْا فِي آياتِنا مُعاجِزِينَ أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ **"As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be forgiveness and a noble provision. But those who strive to foil Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell."** (22:50-51), and others.

By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by His saying: لَهُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ **"They shall have ranks with their Lord"** (8:4) are the levels of proximity (القرب) and nearness (الزلفى) and the ranks of spiritual honor (الكرامة المعنوية). And so it is, for forgiveness and Paradise are certainly among the effects and branches of the levels of proximity to Allah the Glorified.

What the verse contains regarding the establishment of ranks (الدرجات) for these believers is the establishment of all ranks for all of them, not the establishment of all of them for each individual among them. For they are among the concomitants of faith, and faith is varied and possesses levels; thus, the ranks granted in exchange for it are inevitably likewise. Among the believers is he who has one rank, and among them is the possessor of two ranks, and among them is the possessor of many ranks, according to their differing levels in faith.

This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: يَرْفَعِ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجاتٍ **"Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank"** (58:11), and His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَ اللهِ كَمَنْ باءَ بِسَخَطٍ مِنَ اللهِ وَمَأْواهُ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ ، هُمْ دَرَجاتٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَاللهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ **"Is he who follows Allah’s pleasure like him who incurs Allah’s wrath and whose refuge is hell? An evil destination it is! They have [various] ranks with Allah, and Allah watches what they do."** (3:162-163).

From what has preceded, it appears that the interpretation by some of the "ranks" in the verse as the levels of Paradise is not as it should be, and that it is determined that what is meant by them are the ranks of proximity, as mentioned before, even though each of them necessitates the other.

**His saying, the Exalted:** كَما أَخْرَجَكَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَيْتِكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لَكارِهُونَ **"As your Lord brought you out from your home with a true purpose, while a group of the faithful were indeed reluctant"** (8:5) until the end of the two verses. The apparent context is that His saying: كَما أَخْرَجَكَ **"As [He] brought you out"** is connected to what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1). The implied meaning is: Allah judged that the *anfal* belong to Him and His Messenger with the truth (بالحق) despite their dislike for it, just as He brought you out from your home with the truth despite the dislike of a group of them for it. For in all of this, there is a truth that results in a benefit for their religion and their worldly life of which they are heedless.

It was said that it is connected to His saying: يُجادِلُونَكَ فِي الْحَقِ **"disputing with you about the truth"** (8:6). It was also said that the governing agent (العامل) in it is the meaning of "the truth" (الحق), and the implied meaning is: this mention is from the truth just as your Lord brought you out from your home with the truth. Both meanings—as you can see—are far from the context of the verse.

What is meant by **the truth** (الحق) is that which is opposite to falsehood (الباطل); it is the established matter whose actual desired effects result from it. The action—which is the "bringing out"—being "with the truth" means that it was the determined and necessary course according to reality. It was said: what is meant by it is revelation (الوحي); it was said: what is meant by it is *jihad*; and other things were said, but these are distant meanings.

The origin of the meaning of *jadal* (الجدل) — *dispute* — is the tightness of twisting (شدة الفتل). It is said: "a twisted rope" (زمان جديل), meaning tightly twisted. Disputation was called *jadal* because it involves a struggle by twisting [the opponent] away from one doctrine to another, as mentioned in *al-Majma'*.

The meaning of the two verses is: Allah the Exalted judged in the matter of the *anfal* with the truth despite their dislike for His judgment, just as He brought you out from your home in Medina—a bringing out accompanied by the truth—while a group of the believers were indeed reluctant for that, disputing with you about the truth after it had become clear to them in general, while they resembled a group being driven to death while they looked at what had been prepared for them of its causes and instruments.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Jami' al-Jawami'* of al-Tabarsi: Ibn Mas'ud, Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin, al-Baqir, and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them) read: "They ask you [for] the bounties" (يسألونك الأنفال).

**I say:** It was also narrated from Ibn Mas'ud, as well as from al-Sajjad, al-Baqir, and al-Sadiq by others.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from the Righteous Servant (peace be upon him), he said: "**الأنفال كل أرض خربة قد باد أهلها ، وكل أرض لم يوجف عليها بخيل ولا ركاب ـ ولكن صالحوا صلحا وأعطوا بأيديهم على غير قتال ـ فقال ـ : وله ـ يعني الوالي ـ رءوس الجبال وبطون الأودية والآجام ، وكل أرض ميتة لا رب لها ، وله صوافي الملوك : ما كان في أيديهم من غير وجه الغصب لأن الغصب كله مردود ، وهو وارث من لا وارث له ، ويعول من لا حيلة له**" — "The *anfal* are every ruined land whose inhabitants have perished, and every land that was not gained by the charging of horses or camels—but rather they made a peace treaty and surrendered by their own hands without fighting. He said: And to him—meaning the Ruler—belong the peaks of mountains, the depths of valleys, the thickets, and every dead land that has no owner. To him also belong the choice properties of kings: whatever was in their hands not by way of usurpation, for all usurpation must be returned. He is the heir of one who has no heir, and he provides for one who has no means."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ **"They ask you about the bounties"** (8:1), he said: "**من مات وليس له مولى فماله من الأنفال**" — "Whoever dies and has no master [heir], his property is from the *anfal*."

**I say:** Regarding the meaning of these two narrations, there are many narrations reported through the paths of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام). There is no harm in not mentioning the *anfal* in the sense of spoils of war, for the verse by its occasion indicates it, as yielded by its context.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Tayalisi, al-Bukhari in *al-Adab al-Mufrad*, Muslim, al-Nahhas in his *Nasikh*, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Shu'ab* narrated from Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, who said: "Four verses were revealed in the Book of Allah concerning me: My mother had sworn that she would not eat or drink until I left Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so Allah revealed: **وإن جاهداك على أن تشرك بي ما ليس لك به علم ـ فلا تطعهما وصاحبهما في الدنيا معروفا** — 'But if they should strive to make you ascribe to Me that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them, and keep their company worthily in the world.' (31:15).

The second: I had taken a sword that I liked, and I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, grant me this,' so there was revealed: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ **'They ask you about the bounties.'** (8:1).

The third: I fell ill and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came to me. I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I wish to divide my wealth; shall I bequeath half?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'A third?' He remained silent, so the third was permissible after that.

The fourth: I drank wine with a group of the Ansar, and one of them struck my nose with the jawbone of a camel. I went to the Prophet, and Allah revealed the prohibition of wine."

**I say:** The narration is not without issues. Firstly, because His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ جاهَداكَ عَلى أَنْ تُشْرِكَ بِي **"But if they should strive to make you ascribe to Me..."** (31:15) is the conclusion of His saying, the Exalted: وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ **"We have enjoined man concerning his parents"** (31:14), and by its context, it refuses to have been revealed for a specific cause. Furthermore, it has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً **"Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you: that you shall not ascribe any partners to Him, and you shall be good to your parents’"** (6:151) that being good to parents is among the general rulings not specific to one law over another.

Secondly: What was mentioned regarding taking the sword and requesting it from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only suits the recitation: "They ask you [for] the bounties" (يسئلونك الأنفال), not the recitation: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ **"They ask you about the bounties"** (8:1), as clarified in the preceding commentary.

Thirdly: The establishment of the *Sunnah* (السنة) — *the prophetic practice* — regarding the bequest of a third was not by a revealed verse but by a prophetic practice.

Fourthly: The story of his drinking wine with a group of the companions and the wounding of his nose with the jawbone of a camel, although true, occurred while he was drinking wine with a mixed group of the Migrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار), and it was Umar ibn al-Khattab who wounded his nose. Then Allah revealed the verse of *al-Ma'idah*, and it was not revealed for the initial prohibition but for its intensification. All of this has preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ **"O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and azlam are abominations of Satan’s doing"** (5:90).

In the same source: Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Hakim, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from Abu Umamah, who said: "I asked 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit about the *anfal*, and he said: 'It was revealed concerning us, the companions of Badr, when we differed over the spoils (النفل) and our conduct regarding it became poor. So Allah took it out of our hands and placed it with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) divided it among the Muslims equally (عن براء says: عن سواء).'"

In the same source: Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Hakim—who authenticated it—al-Bayhaqi, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit, who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and I witnessed Badr with him. The people met and Allah defeated the enemy. A group went out in pursuit of them as they fled, killing them; a group fell upon the camp, seizing and collecting it; and a group surrounded the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that the enemy would not find an opening to strike him. When it was night and the people returned to one another, those who collected the spoils said: 'We seized and collected them, so no one has a share in them.' Those who went out in pursuit of the enemy said: 'You are not more entitled to them than us; we drove the enemy away from them and defeated them.' Those who surrounded the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'You are not more entitled than us; we surrounded the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) fearing that the enemy might find an opening to strike him, and we were occupied with him.' So there was revealed: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَصْلِحُوا ذاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ **'They ask you about the bounties. Say, “The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle.” So be wary of Allah and settle your differences'** (8:1). So the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) divided them among the Muslims." (The tradition).

In the same source: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Abu Dawud, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Hibban, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Hakim—who authenticated it—and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "When it was the day of Badr, the Prophet said: '**من قتل قتيلا فله كذا وكذا ـ ومن أسر أسيرا فله كذا وكذا**' — 'Whoever kills a person shall have such-and-such, and whoever takes a captive shall have such-and-such.' As for the elders, they remained under the banners, while the youths rushed toward the killing and the spoils. The elders said to the youths: 'Include us with you, for we were a support (ردءاً) for you, and had anything happened to you, you would have sought refuge with us.' They disputed before the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so there was revealed: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **'They ask you about the bounties. Say, “The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle.”'** (8:1). So he divided the spoils among them equally."

**I say:** There are other narrations with these meanings, and there are narrations indicating the details of the story by which the meaning of the verses becomes clear; we will mention them in the commentary on the following verses.

In some narrations, it is mentioned that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) promised them that he would give them the personal effects (السلب) and the spoils, then Allah the Exalted abrogated that with His saying: قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1). This is what is alluded to in this narration, and for this reason, it was sometimes said that it is not obligatory for the Imam to fulfill what he promised to the combatants. However, this is made unlikely by their dispute over the matter of spoils on the day of Badr, for had the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) promised them that, they would not have differed in the face of his explicit statement.

In the same source: Ibn Jarir narrated from Mujahid: "They asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about the fifth (الخمس) after the four-fifths, so there was revealed: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ **'They ask you about the bounties.'** (8:1)."

**I say:** This does not align with what has preceded of the verse's content as yielded by the context. In some of what has been reported from the early commentators like Sa'id ibn Jubayr, Mujahid, and 'Ikrimah, as well as from Ibn Abbas, it is said that His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ **"They ask you about the bounties. Say, ‘The bounties belong to Allah and the Apostle’"** (8:1) is abrogated by His saying: وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ **"Know that whatever thing you may come by as spoil, a fifth of it belongs to Allah and to the Apostle"** (8:41). It has already preceded in the commentary on the verse that which negates the possibility of abrogation.

In the same source: Malik, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Abu 'Ubayd, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, al-Nahhas, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Qasim ibn Muhammad, who said: "I heard a man asking Ibn Abbas about the *anfal*, and he said: 'The horse is from the *nafal* and the personal effects are from the *nafal*.' The man repeated the question, and Ibn Abbas said that again.

Then the man said: 'The *anfal* that Allah mentioned in His Book, what are they?' He did not stop asking him until he almost annoyed him. Ibn Abbas said: 'This one is like Sabigh whom Umar struck.' In another wording: 'How much you need someone to strike you as Umar did to Sabigh the Iraqi!' Umar had struck him until blood flowed down his heels."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا **"It is they who are truly the faithful"** (8:4), al-Tabarani narrated from al-Harith ibn Malik al-Ansari that he passed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said to him: "How have you reached the morning, O Harith?" He said: "I have reached the morning as a true believer." He said: "Look at what you say, for every thing has a reality (حقيقة); so what is the reality of your faith?" He said: "I have turned my soul away from the world, so I have kept my night awake and made my day thirsty. It is as if I am looking at the people of Paradise visiting one another therein, and as if I am looking at the people of the Fire howling therein." He said: "O Harith, you have recognized, so remain firm," three times.

**I say:** The tradition is narrated through the paths of the Shi'a with numerous chains of narration.
